steps.title: Find the bottleneck
steps.content: The passage identifies the problem in microbial fuel cells: electrons produced by bacteria must travel through inefficient redox reactions before reaching the outside electrode. That slow transfer keeps typical power density at or below 0.30 mW/cm2. After researchers added silver nanoparticles to the anode covering, power density rose to 0.66 mW/cm2.

steps.title: Use the conductivity clue
steps.content: The last sentence points to silver's high electrical conductivity, so the hypothesis should explain the increase through easier electron movement. Choice A shifts the explanation to bacterial metabolism, but the passage locates the bottleneck after electrons are produced. Choice B removes the nanoparticles from the explanation. Choice D says silver catalyzes the redox reactions, which is not the conductivity-based mechanism.

steps.title: Choose direct transfer
steps.content: Choice C fits the experiment and the stated property of silver: conductive nanoparticles could let electrons bypass the inefficient redox chain and move directly to the electrode. That mechanism explains why adding silver would increase power output. The answer is C.
